name: Ordás and Tapia Loop from Sel
name_original: Ordás y Tapia – circular desde el embalse de Selga de Ordás
description: A moderate 21.3-mile touring cycling route around the Selga de Ordás reservoir, featuring scenic riparian landscapes and historical towers.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Carrocera offers routes through the naturally rich region of Castile and León, characterized by varied topography. The area features mountainous landscapes, including the vicinity of Peña Ubiña, and routes along water bodies such as the Barrios de Luna Reservoir. A network of rural roads connects smaller towns, providing diverse terrain for cyclists. These routes allow exploration of the region's natural features and rural settings. Best touring cycling routes around Carrocera The most popular touring cycling route is Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve Loop, a 32.5 miles (52.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 50 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the diverse landscapes of the Biosphere Reserve. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Ordás and Tapia Loop from Sel, a moderate 21.4 miles (34.4 km) path. This route offers a scenic loop through rural areas, often completed in about 2 hours 19 minutes. Local touring cyclists also love the Santa María de Ordás and Las Omañas Loop, a 18.5 miles (29.8 km) trail leading through rural towns and varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 26 minutes. Touring cycling around Carrocera is defined by mountainous landscapes, rural roads, and valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging routes. The routes in Carrocera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 200 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Carrocera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Carrocera?
A: There are over 25 touring cycling routes in the Carrocera area, offering a diverse range of options for cyclists. These routes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Carrocera?
A: Yes, Carrocera offers routes suitable for various skill levels. For an easier ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10504177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Robla Town Center – Ermita del Buen Suceso loop from La Pola de Gordón&lt;/a&gt;. This easy 18.8 km (11.7 miles) path takes about 1 hour 18 minutes and features minimal elevation gain, making it a good option for those seeking a relaxed experience.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Carrocera?
A: The region around Carrocera is characterized by varied topography, including mountainous landscapes and rural roads. You'll find routes with significant elevation changes, such as the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007763" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Muria mountain pass – View of Llombera loop from Pola de Gordón&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 700 meters of ascent. Other routes offer more moderate terrain, often following valleys and connecting small towns.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the Carrocera area?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Carrocera are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037556746" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ordás and Tapia Loop from Sel&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1363111172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa María de Ordás and Las Omañas Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering scenic circular journeys through rural areas.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while cycling in the Carrocera area?
A: The Carrocera region is rich in natural beauty. You can cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4073598" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Calderones Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, discover the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5548992" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barrios de Luna Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, or even encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall of the Arroyo de los Calderones&lt;/a&gt;. The area also features beautiful forests like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5741568" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hayedo de la Boyariza&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the routes often pass through or near charming rural towns and offer glimpses of local heritage. You might encounter the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of Manadero in the Cave of the Doves&lt;/a&gt;, a religious building nestled within a cave, providing a cultural highlight during your ride.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Carrocera?
A: Given its location in Castile and León, the spring and autumn months generally offer the most pleasant conditions for touring cycling in Carrocera. Temperatures are milder, and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be warm, especially in the valleys, while winter may bring colder temperatures and potential snow in higher mountainous areas like those near Peña Ubiña.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a touring cycling route in Carrocera?
A: While specific parking areas for each route are not detailed, the presence of numerous smaller towns like Benllera, Piedrasecha, and La Pola de Gordon suggests that parking is generally available in these rural centers. Many routes, being loops, will start and end in a village where you can typically find suitable parking.

Q: Is public transport available to access touring cycling routes in Carrocera?
A: Public transport options in this rural region may be limited. While some larger towns might have bus services, accessing specific trailheads often requires personal transport. It's advisable to check local bus schedules if you plan to rely on public transport to reach your starting point.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Carrocera?
A: The touring cycling routes around Carrocera are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an impressive average rating of 4.8 out of 5 stars from over 8 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, from challenging mountain passes to tranquil rural roads, and the opportunity to explore the natural beauty of the region.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Absolutely. Carrocera is home to many difficult routes, with 22 out of 29 routes categorized as such. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037344610" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, covering 52.3 km (32.5 miles) with over 1000 meters of elevation gain, offering a substantial challenge for experienced touring cyclists.

Q: Can I find viewpoints or mountain passes along the touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the mountainous terrain around Carrocera features several notable passes and viewpoints. You can cycle over the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5470451" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de la Camparona&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6335590" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aralla Pass&lt;/a&gt;, which offer panoramic views of the surrounding landscapes and valleys.
tips: With only about twenty inhabitants, Irián is a quiet place that few pass by anymore, but it is the typical place that is worth visiting with a bike, not so much for its architecture but to appreciate its peaceful heartbeat.

Curious circular tower with a fireplace and everything! It is quite well preserved and until recently it was possible to access its interior, which is also amazing.

You can read more about the tower and the legends that surround it in this interesting article: https://www.eldiario.es/castilla-y-leon/provincias/leon/ruta-tierra-torreon-santa-maria-ordas-leyenda -don-ares-omana_1_6114155.html

In Tapia de la Ribera it is worth going to its tower, which was the subject of a dispute between Gonzalo de Tapia and the Count of Luna in 1543 in a lawsuit before the Chancilleria de Valladolid. The tower had been built two centuries earlier by the Marqueses of Astorga (the Osorios) to control the Órbigo-Luna valley.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Ordas y Tapia circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily paved, offering a smooth ride for touring cyclists. While most of the 34.4-kilometer path is accessible, there are minimal segments that might be unpaved or slightly more challenging. This makes it suitable for a wide range of cyclists.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners or families with children?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is rated as moderate and its predominantly paved surfaces make it quite inviting for beginners and families. While a good level of fitness is beneficial, the lack of extreme technical demands ensures an enjoyable experience for most.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Ordas y Tapia cycling route?
faqs.answer: You'll enjoy picturesque views centered around the Selga de Ordás reservoir and the Luna River valley. Historical highlights include passing through Irián Village, seeing the Tower of Santa María de Ordás, and the Tower of Tapia de la Ribera, which is part of 14th-century castle ruins.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Selga de Ordás reservoir cycling trail?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, the route is a circular one starting and ending at the Selga de Ordás reservoir. It's advisable to look for parking facilities near the reservoir itself or in the nearby villages of Ordás or Tapia de la Ribera.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region of Castilla y León generally offers pleasant cycling conditions during spring and autumn, with milder temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring colder weather, so checking seasonal weather forecasts is recommended for optimal enjoyment.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Ordas y Tapia cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the trail itself is primarily paved and passes through rural areas, specific regulations regarding dogs are not provided. It's generally a good practice to keep dogs on a leash, especially when passing through villages or near livestock, and to ensure they are comfortable with cycling speeds and distances.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Ordas y Tapia circular route. It's an open public route through the natural and historical landscapes of the León province.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, pubs, or accommodation options near the Selga de Ordás reservoir?
faqs.answer: The route passes through rural areas and villages like Irián, Ordás, and Tapia de la Ribera. While specific establishments are not listed, these villages are likely to offer some local amenities such as small cafes or guesthouses. It's a good idea to research options in advance, especially for accommodation.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Camino Olvidado (La Robla-Cirujales) for about 2% of its length, and also touches upon the Antigüo camino a Benllera and Calle de la Fuente for very small segments.

faqs.question: What kind of natural features can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers scenic views of the Selga de Ordás reservoir, which is a central feature. You'll also experience the riparian landscapes of the Luna River valley, characteristic of the natural beauty found in the Castilla y León region.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the starting point of the route?
faqs.answer: Information on direct public transport to the Selga de Ordás reservoir or the immediate starting points is not readily available. Access is typically by private vehicle. You may find regional bus services to larger towns in the León province, from which you would need to arrange further transport.
